research_article.title: Practitioner's Guide to Preseason - 2025 Edition
research_article.key_findings: Comprehensive preseason planning guide covering periodization principles, progressive loading strategies, baseline testing protocols, and athlete readiness monitoring. Emphasizes balancing fitness development with skill work while managing injury risk through appropriate load progression.
practical_applications: Manage training loads with ACWR monitoring

knowledge_base_entries.question: How should preseason training be structured and periodized?
knowledge_base_entries.answer: Preseason training follows periodization principles with distinct phases: general preparation (building base fitness), specific preparation (sport-specific development), and competition preparation (tapering and sharpening). Volume starts high and decreases while intensity progressively increases. Include strength, power, speed, and conditioning work integrated with technical-tactical training.
knowledge_base_entries.summary: Preseason periodization progressively builds from general preparation through specific preparation to competition readiness.

knowledge_base_entries.question: What testing should be conducted during preseason?
knowledge_base_entries.answer: Preseason testing establishes baselines for strength (force plates, isometric testing), power (jumps, throws), speed (sprint times), and aerobic capacity. Include injury risk screening (movement quality, asymmetries). Testing identifies individual strengths, weaknesses, and training priorities. Repeat key tests mid-season to track adaptation.
knowledge_base_entries.summary: Comprehensive preseason testing establishes baselines and identifies individual training needs across multiple physical qualities.
best_practices: Test strength, power, speed, and endurance
Establish individual training priorities from results

knowledge_base_entries.question: How should training load be managed during preseason?
knowledge_base_entries.answer: Preseason load management requires monitoring acute:chronic workload ratios (ACWR), staying in the 0.8-1.3 range. Progress loads by no more than 10% per week. Monitor session RPE, wellness markers, and readiness scores. Include planned deload weeks. Individualize based on training age and injury history. Avoid load spikes that increase injury risk.
knowledge_base_entries.summary: Preseason load management uses ACWR monitoring and progressive loading (10% rule) to build capacity while managing injury risk.
best_practices: Progress loads by maximum 10% per week
Track session RPE and wellness markers
Individualize based on athlete training history
Balance team and individual training needs
